2605.20086.title: What Do Evolutionary Coding Agents Evolve?
2605.20086.paper_summary: Recent work pairs LLMs with evolutionary search to iteratively generate, modify, and select code using task-specific feedback. These systems have produced strong results in mathematical discovery and algorithm design, yet a fundamental question remains: what do they actually evolve? Progress is typically summarized by the best score a run reaches under a task-specific evaluator, but that score can reflect several different mechanisms: new algorithmic structure, re-tuning an existing strategy, recombining ideas already in the model's internal knowledge, or overfitting to the evaluator. Distinguishing these mechanisms requires inspecting the search process itself, not only its final outcome. We introduce EvoTrace, a dataset of evolutionary coding traces spanning four evolutionary frameworks, reasoning and non-reasoning models, and 16 tasks across mathematics and algorithm design. To analyze these traces, we develop EvoReplay, a replay-based methodology that reconstructs the local search states behind high-scoring solutions and tests controlled interventions, including adjusting constants, removing program components and substituting models or prompting contexts. We annotate every code edit in EvoTrace with one of nine recurring edit types using an LLM-as-judge pipeline validated against blind human re-annotation. Across EvoTrace, most score gains come from a small subset of these edit types. We further find a deterministic cycling pattern: about 30% of code lines added during search are byte-identical re-introductions of previously-deleted lines, present throughout nearly every run. These results show that benchmark gains in evolutionary coding agents can arise from qualitatively different mechanisms, only some of which correspond to new algorithmic structure. EvoTrace enables more diagnostic evaluation of evolutionary coding agents beyond final benchmark scores.
2605.20086.paper_summary_zh: 最近的研究將大型語言模型（LLMs）與進化搜索相結合，通過任務特定的反饋迭代生成、修改和選擇代碼。這些系統在數學發現和算法設計方面取得了強勁的成果，但仍然存在一個根本性問題：它們實際上在進化什麼？進展通常通過運行在任務特定評估器下達到的最佳分數來總結，但該分數可以反映幾種不同的機制：新的算法結構、重新調整現有策略、重新組合已在模型內部知識中的想法，或對評估器的過擬合。區分這些機制需要檢查搜索過程本身，而不僅僅是其最終結果。我們介紹了EvoTrace，這是一個跨越四個進化框架、推理和非推理模型，以及數學和算法設計中的16個任務的進化編碼痕跡數據集。為了分析這些痕跡，我們開發了EvoReplay，一種基於回放的方法論，重建高分解決方案背後的局部搜索狀態並測試控制干預，包括調整常數、刪除程序組件和替換模型或提示上下文。我們使用經過盲人重新註釋驗證的LLM作為評判者的管道，對EvoTrace中的每個代碼編輯進行了九種類型的重複編輯類型的註釋。在EvoTrace中，大多數分數增益來自這些編輯類型的一小部分。我們進一步發現了一種確定性循環模式：在搜索過程中添加的約30%的代碼行是之前刪除行的字節相同的重新引入，幾乎在每次運行中都存在。這些結果顯示，進化編碼代理的基準增益可以來自質量不同的機制，其中只有一部分與新的算法結構相對應。EvoTrace使得對進化編碼代理的診斷評估超越了最終基準分數。
2605.20086.author: Nico Pelleriti et.al.
2605.20086.authors: Nico Pelleriti, Sree Harsha Nelaturu, Zhanke Zhou, Zongze Li, Max Zimmer, Bo Han, Sebastian Pokutta
